title: Release It!
subtitle: Design and Deploy Production-Ready Software
author: Michael T. Nygard
description: A single dramatic software failure can cost millions — but can be avoided with simple changes to design and architecture. This book shows how to create systems that run longer, fail less, and recover better.
themes: Software must be designed for the hostile reality of production, not the safety of development
Stability comes from intentional architectural patterns, not heroic debugging
Integration points are the #1 source of production failures
Failures cascade through resource exhaustion — isolate and contain them
Operations is not an afterthought; it must be architected from day one
Cynical software expects everything to break and is never surprised
awards: Widely considered the definitive work on production-ready software
Popularized the Circuit Breaker pattern across the software industry
excerpt: A single dramatic software failure can cost a company millions of dollars — but can be avoided with simple changes to design and architecture. This new edition of the best-selling industry standard shows you how to create systems that run longer, with fewer failures, and recover better when bad things happen.
keyIdeas: Integration points are the most dangerous part of any system — every call to another system is a potential failure
Cascading failures propagate through resource exhaustion: blocked threads drain pools, taking down healthy components
Circuit Breaker prevents wasted work by failing fast when a dependency is unhealthy, then testing recovery
Bulkheads partition system resources so failure in one compartment cannot sink the whole ship
Timeouts are the simplest and most effective stability pattern — always set them everywhere
Handshaking lets backends tell frontends to throttle, preventing overload death spirals
Fail Fast: reserve resources only after validation, so you don't waste capacity on doomed requests
Systems must be cynical — expect network failures, slow responses, corrupted data, and malicious traffic
keyTakeaways: Use Circuit Breakers around every integration point to prevent cascading failures
Apply Timeouts to every outbound call — unbounded waits are the leading cause of thread exhaustion
Partition resources with Bulkheads so a failure in one feature doesn't take down unrelated features
Implement Load Shedding and Handshaking to handle traffic spikes gracefully
Design for zero-downtime deployments and continuous delivery from the start
Monitor at two levels: internal health checks AND external synthetic transactions
Run chaos experiments (disaster simulations) to find weaknesses before they find you
Don't chase 100% test coverage — chase production resilience instead
whoShouldRead: Software developers who want to stop getting paged at 3 AM
Architects designing distributed systems that must survive real-world traffic
DevOps and SRE engineers responsible for production uptime
Engineering leaders building resilient platforms and services
